figures.caption: Figure 2: Constraint distribution per program, sorted by number of violations. Constraints are classified by violation counts: discriminative (e f>0, ep=0), over-approximate (e f>0, ep>0), and irrelevant (e f=0). Most programs contain violated constraints, with over half being discriminative.

Table 4: Semantic index region ablation. Coverage: programs with at least one violated constraint in the region. Discrim. %: fraction of violated constraints that are discriminative. ∆Acc@3/5: performance drop when the region is removed. Wt.: granularity weight in scoring.

Table 6: SEMLOC on real-world BUGSINPY bugs (youtube-dl, n=7), built on top of AutoFL as the function-level backbone. Rank: position of ground-truth faulty line in SEMLOC’s suspiciousness ordering. %Susp: fraction of executable lines flagged suspicious (lower is better). ✓/×: whether the faulty line appears in the top-k ranked lines.

Table 2: SemFault-250 benchmark statistics by application domain. Avg. Lines is the mean program length (buggy version); Avg. Tests is the mean test-suite size; Avg. Fail is the mean number of tests that fail on the buggy version.
Table 1: Constraint categories used by SEMLOC. The set is used as the label space for constraint inference and indexing.

Table 5: Semantic constraint distribution on 250 programs. Constraints are classified as discriminative (failing only), over-approximate (both), or irrelevant (never triggered). Discriminative constraints are further divided into primary and secondary based on their contribution to localization.

Table 3: Fault localization on SemFault-250. Acc@k: fraction of programs with the faulty line in the top-k (higher is better). %Susp: fraction of lines flagged as suspicious (lower is better). Rank: median rank of the faulty line (lower is better). SEMLOC † disables semantic indexing. SEMLOC + removes counterfactual reasoning.
